column3: Threat model assumes passive attackers can read packets from the network

column5: Informational section; QUIC's per-connection authentication mitigates path tampering

column3: QUIC assumes the threat model described in [SEC-CONS]
column5: QUIC implementation uses GnuTLS with TLS 1.3 and AEAD encryption (AES-128-GCM for Initial, negotiated cipher for other levels)

column3: Handshake security properties must be ensured
column5: Handshake implemented in crypt.c using GnuTLS TLS 1.3 with QUIC transport parameters extension; key derivation per RFC 9001

column3: Protected packets must ensure confidentiality and integrity
column5: Implemented in crypt.c: header protection via AES-ECB sample masking (YAWT_q_crypto_unprotect_header/protect_packet) and AEAD decryption/encryption (YAWT_q_crypto_decrypt_payload/protect_packet)

column3: Connection migration security properties must be ensured
column5: PATH_CHALLENGE/PATH_RESPONSE frames implemented (quic.c lines 627-633, quic_connection.c line 522) for path validation; NEW_CONNECTION_ID and RETIRE_CONNECTION_ID frames implemented for CID management

column3: Packet number replay protection (Section 12.3) is part of security properties
column5: Reconstructed PNs compared against largest_seen per level in quic_connection.c line 636; duplicates silently dropped

column3: Security properties of handshake, protected packets, and connection migration considered separately
column5: Informational statement organizing the section; no single code feature corresponds

column3: Crypto buffer size limits to prevent resource exhaustion during handshake
column5: max_crypto_buffer_bytes policy enforced in crypt.c lines 536-541 with CRYPTO_BUFFER_EXCEEDED error

column3: QUIC provides encrypted/authenticated transport protecting against DoS
column5: YAWT uses gnutls AEAD for packet protection (protect/unprotect functions)

column3: After handshake, endpoints discard unauthenticated packets
column5: YAWT_q_crypto_unprotect_packet returns error on auth failure, packet is skipped

column3: Unauthenticated ICMP packet acceptance is extremely limited after connection established
column5: No ICMP handling found in YAWT codebase

column3: Stateless reset is the only other packet type accepted outside authenticated packets

column3: During connection creation, only protection against off-network-path attacks
column5: Initial keys derived from DCID provides some protection but no explicit off-path vs on-path distinction

column3: All QUIC packets contain proof that recipient saw a preceding packet from peer

column3: Addresses cannot change during handshake; endpoints can discard packets on different network path
column5: YAWT does not check address consistency during handshake; peer_addr is stored but not validated

column3: Source and Destination Connection IDs are primary protection against off-path attack

column5: Server stores odcid hash for pre-handshake matching; main CID hash for post-handshake

column5: No version negotiation packet parsing or handling found in codebase

column3: Servers SHOULD limit the usage and lifetime of address validation tokens to prevent amplification attacks via token reuse with spoofed 0-RTT connections
column5: No address validation token management system exists; NEW_TOKEN frame (0x07) is declared in types enum but has no parsing, encoding, or enqueue implementation, and no token expiration or single-use enforcement is present

column3: 0-RTT packet parsing/encoding stubs exist but actual 0-RTT data flow and early key establishment are not implemented, so the specific attack vector is not exploitable; however, the protocol requirement for token usage/lifetime limits is not addressed

column3: Optimistic ACK attack is described as an attack where an endpoint that acknowledges packets it has not received may cause a congestion controller to permit excessive sending rates
column5: The RFC section only describes the attack concept; YAWT has no specific code referencing this attack.

column3: Endpoints MAY skip packet numbers when sending to detect optimistic ACK behavior
column5: No packet number skipping or gap-insertion mechanism exists in YAWT send path; no such detection logic found anywhere in src, include, tests, or examples.

column3: On detecting optimistic ACK, endpoint may close connection with PROTOCOL_VIOLATION error code
column5: PROTOCOL_VIOLATION (0x0A) is not defined as a named constant in YAWT, and no code closes connections with this specific error code in response to ACK anomalies.
